comments.title: Completed Dolmen Arch Lesser Mysteries and Sharing Eirik Westcoat's American Futharch
comments.content:
Good Evening,

If you're an American, I hope you're enjoying the long weekend!

To Share: 1. Well, it only took me 2 years, but I have completed the Lesser Mysteries of the Dolmen Arch course, and have begun the Gradd y Gwyddon y Ffordd! I'm excited at what I have already learned, and to see what unfolds as I keep along the path. Many thanks, JMG, for putting together this remarkable course.

2. I wrote up a short post in response to what strikes me as a very interesting insight from Eirik Westcoat about the origins of the Runes and why they have the order they do. Also, he made up a new Futharch custom tailored to modern American English, with full intention of it working for meditation, magic, and divination (he's a master of the Rune Gild, so there's a least a decent chance he got that right): https://jpowellrussell.com/#eirik_westcoat_and_the_american_futharch

No question tonight, as I'd like to focus on my gratitude above.

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Completed Dolmen Arch Lesser Mysteries and Sharing Eirik Westcoat's American Futharch
replies.content:
Bert,

1) Thanks very much! Reading over the chapter, it's a bit daunting, but I suppose that's why I've been spending the last few years learning the value of "one day of steady practice at a time."

2) While I'm sure it's worth thinking and/or meditating on, in case this was something you didn't know (and if you already knew, my apologies): the 5 sounds that don't have a single rune are all "dipthongs," which is what you get when you say two vowel sounds in a blended way. For example, in "sail," you say the sounds "eh" and "eye" smashed together to get the sound we've learned to associate with "ai" (in this instance!). Linguists distinguish the two vowels as still being distinct because of where the tongue is in the mouth and how open the jaw is, but acknowledge that they run together, rather than being two totally distinct sounds, like in "re-emerge," where two vowels are next to each other but not blended. Westcoat's approach was to honor the distinctness of the sounds by retaining their individual Runes, while providing the diphthongs as examples of common blended sounds since most of us aren't used to noticing that diphthongs are blends of two sounds unless we've taken some linguistics classes.

As for what esoteric or numerlogical significance might be there, I haven't put any work in yet.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: American Gods
replies.content:
Well, the American Revolution wasn't really a social or ideological revolution; I'd go so far as so say that the term "revolution" here is almost a misnomer. It was more a war of secession (from the British Empire). Otherwise all the preexisting social mores, customs, and institutions (sans the British control) of the American colonies stayed more or less intact after the war, plus the addition of the new US constitution and government organs.

The typical revisionist historical narrative we're used to seeing might brandish claims like the Americans getting rid of the monarchy was such a revolutionary act! But that was already done during the English Civil War, and the crown was never quite the same after that. The firmly-restored British monarchy was permanently de-fanged in the aftermath of the so-called "Glorious" Revolution of 1688. Nay, the American colonies seceded from an imperial parliament. What the US accomplished was IMHO a continuation of events that had been going on in England for about 150 years.

I'm not sure the land spirits are going to save us from the emerging managerial tyranny that now openly mocks and derides our founders and the constitution. There was an op-ed headline the other day in the NYT that openly called the constitution problematic and an impediment to the neoliberal managerial regime's vision of "democracy." The American people becoming more in tune with the land might help things a lot though!

replies.title: Re: American Gods
replies.content:
My comment is based on a passing familiarity with Santeria (I attended a few private rituals performed by a babalao who was a friend of a friend and read a few books about Santeria and other Afro-Diasporic religions.)

By "son", I merely mean that JFK had enough in common with the stories about Shango that I suspect that Shango had his head.

Road: in Voudoun, and I believe in Santeria and Lucumi, loas/Orishas manifest in a number of ways which are related but different enough that if they were human, they would be different people. There are ways that Ancient Greek religion and West African polytheist religions are similar. This is one of them. Many of the important Greek deities are identified by a proper name followed by an epithet, which is (usually) an adjective. Artemis of Ephesus is a Great Mother, Artemis Orthia (the Upright or Correct Artemis) was worshipped in Sparta with a ritual ordeal; the Athenian Artemis was/is the familiar maiden huntress who protects young children, is associated with bears, and has a covey of lesbian nymphs for companions. These look like different goddesses to us, but in ancient times were either several figures who got merged under the same name or the same being who takes on different aspects in different locations.

If you look up the loa Erzulie, you see the same thing. Erzulie is associated with sexual love, but presents herself differently in personality and interests in a way that has been consistent enough that they have different identifiers so you know which one to invoke.

Sometimes people who do something impressive enough in their lifetimes to be not only remembered but venerated after their death become semi-divine. I believe that is what the ancient Greeks meant when they called someone a hero. Some Catholic saints have stories that put them on the borderline between a historical person and a disguised pagan deity. Voudoun brings this process full circle by treating a few saints as aspects of loa. I read somewhere that the historical Caribbean pirate Anne Bonney wound up being worshipped as an Orisha after her death.

I'm speculating that JFK made enough of a cultural impression that he might wind up dentified as a manifestation of Shango.

I got rid of all the autocorrection I could catch.

I had held off for fear it was too obvious, but since I haven't seen anyone mention the more prominent founding fathers, who would seem to qualify for at least "saint" status in such an approach, especially those who have developed their own mythology/tall tales, like Washington and Franklin.

The other main thought that comes to mind that I haven't seen anyone else mention is the Transcendentalist and related movements. A bit light on "deities," but figures like Emerson and Walt Whitman definitely seem to have been channeling a sense of the divine found in this land and people. Maybe better for "vibes" than specific figures of honor or veneration.

Lastly, and possibly a bit silly/off the mark, there are also the popular characters of the various holidays, sometimes ostensibly Christian, and/or with European roots of one kind or another, who have become distinct cultural touchpoints - Santa, Rudolf, the Easter Bunny, Tooth Fairy, and so forth. These might not be serious enough or too wrapped up in consumerism for worship, but I thought I'd mention them as figures that have taken root in the collective consciousness for better or worse.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends on the vaccine, I'd think.
The smallpox vaccine was a preindustrial product. Pasteur's rabies vaccine was from the industrial revolution, but as I recall the lab equipment would have been available to any aspiring alchemist. Measels, mumps and rubella are all Pasteur-style vaccines, and so probably have reasonable energy footprints as well. (Culturing virus in eggs is easy enough with passive solar heat in most places.)

The tetanus, pertussis and diphtheria vaccines aren't vaccines against those bacteria, but against the toxic metabolites they produce. (Those bugs themselves won't kill you directly, it's their poop that poisons you-- that's also why they're always circulating.) All you need to produce them is the ability to culture the bacteria and isolate the toxin for inactivation-- a centrifuge will do, which can be human-powered. Inactivation is again, just heat and not a lot of it.

In these cases, the energy requirements are much lower than the knowledge requirements. Can we keep those going? Given the current crisis of competency literally everywhere, I honestly am not sure. You lose a lot in a dark age, even things your society ought to theoretically be able to produce. In one, Greece lost writing; in another, England lost the potter's wheel. We may very well lose vaccination for reasons totally unrelated to the energy requirements.

I'm thinking I ought to offer to Epona that someone reinvent horse serum. (Many of the things we vaccinate against-- and some we can't vaccinate against-- can be treated with antiserum). I got the impression that serum production can be a good retirement for a workhorse, and I imagine if they become known as life-givers it would rather up the status of those animals in our successor cultures. Something like cows to a Hindu-- I can't see any horse deity objecting to that.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings everyone.

JMG, you discussed archetypes a couple of MMs before, where you said they were the psychic reflections of the instinct patterns that we use to look at reality so to speak, and the "noeta" are the external mental objects, perceptions (perhaps even spirits?).

I wanted to ask you to share more about the self archetype. Does the non-dual experience / loss of narrativity that happens after a kundalini awakening come from it? Is it the same thing as keter or tipereth on the tree of life? How exactly does it activate and work? Is the full activate the end of material incarnation?

Lastly, how does the god image relate to the self if the god image arises out of the base archetypes first?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) You're running a lot of semi-related and unrelated things together here. The archetype of the Self is simply the psychic reflection of the instinct-pattern for wholeness -- it's the archetype that manifests itself through any form of unity of being or wholeness of self. That can happen in many different ways and on many different levels, and no two people experience its activation in the same way. The one of these questions I can answer simply is that the archetype of the self is Tiphareth. The three supernals, and Kether above all, cannot be defined or approached or understood by any created being.

2) The image is not the reality. The image the god takes on arises from the archetype, but who created the archetype?

Hi. I did some work with JM's system about ten or so years ago. I was on her private email list for awhile (now closed in favor of fb :( ... I did the work in the first book, Magical Knowledge I: The Apprentice with good effect, and some of Magical Knowledge II: The Initiate as well. I started work on the Quariea course when it came out and made some headway through the first two and half modules (about 25 lessons approximately) when I guess you could say I had a karmic culmination.

The personal crisis that ensued caused me to stop practicing magic for a few years, and instead I gave up my alcohol use, which had been problematic as a teen. I kept up with prayer through a difficult period -in fact I owe prayer as to guiding me through the difficulty. I tried to stay away from the occult, but I couldn't, I'd been into it since I was a kid in some ways. So... when I came back it was slowly with New Thought materials from Emmett Fox and then the Order of Essenes material, and when the Golden Section Fellowship came out I had intuition it would work for my current state on the path. I still have in mind finishing the Quareia course at some point, but for now the GSF work fits in better and meshes with my outer life very well -and it is going well, if I can judge by the changes that I and others have seen in my life.

The visionary & ritual in Quareia work can be quite demanding and intense, so I think part of it is to do with the different approaches -The GSF being on the "occult" path and Quareia being more firmly within the realm of "magic" i.e. ritual or ceremonial magic.

All of my personal interactions with Josephine have been great -in the form of email, and comments on her blog and such. She gave me some great one-to-one guidance and I consider her a teacher even if I didn't finish the Quareia course. Also, her husband Stuart Littlejohn is a fantsatic painter and I've used some of the images in his book "Sacred Woman" as a focal point for at least one of the deities I work with, and now am using another.

If you feel called by it and it fits with your life, I'd say its worth exploring and reading at least. She has done some Great Work and made in-my-opinion a very good contribution to the continuing revival of magic.

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Regardie's work isn't primary. He had a collection of papers from the Stella Matutina, one of the GD's successor orders, and he constructed the Big Gray Brick out of that raw material. The knowledge lectures, now finally available in a complete and unedited collection, are primary, but most of the people who have copies of the rest of the original papers are sitting on them and refusing to let anyone else have access to them. When I was editing Regardie's book, I sent out an open letter inviting people to contribute to an improved version, and got very, very little response -- and next to none from those who have original sources. It was frankly sad. (And of course some of those people are now sniping at my edition of the book online, insisting that it's inadequate. Well, folks, you had your chance to help...)

R.G. Torrens' The Secret Rituals of the Golden Dawn and The Inner Teachings of the Golden Dawn are also secondary sources based on original texts, but they're worth comparing to Regardie's work.

One set of sources that almost nobody pays attention to are the Collectanea Hermetica, a set of short books edited by Westcott and studied by GD members. You can download the whole set free of charge here:

https://archive.org/search?query=collectanea+hermetica

I've been told that there's an edition of the original 1888 rituals in production -- those are very different from the ones Regardie used -- and that will be a major contribution once it appears. I'd like, though, to see someone just dump the whole kit and caboodle online, or turn it into a big anthology and publish it, instead of squatting on it all like a toad on a cow turd and thinking that makes them important.

(Yes, I know. I get grumpy about this subject.)

replies.title: Re: Platonic Solids
replies.content:
I haven't read a specific biography of either, but some biographical details are covered in Jon Peterson's magesterial Playing at the World. At a minimum, both were up to their eyeballs in early-to-mid 20th century pulp, fantasy, and sci-fi, and much of that fantasy was heavily influenced by Theosophy, if nothing else (our host has mentioned Howard's familiarity there, and Howard was one of the major influences on D&D). Gygax was also a Jehovah's Witness, with varying levels of devoutness throughout his life, so he might have been somewhat familiar with alternative spirituality through that. The other confirmed path of possible influence is that Gygax mined medieval bestiaries and like books for monster ideas. Sylphs, Naiads, Salamanders, and Gnomes even show up very early on, alongside more generic "elementals"!

I don't know that I've ever read this anywhere, but my current guess is that Gygax, being the voracious reader he was, and more than happy to pillage anything not nailed down as inspiration for adventure material, was at least familiar with the Theosophy that was still popular when he was a kid, and of which there were likely still echoes, especially amongst the geeky circles he ran around in, by the time he and Areneson put D&D together. As evidence, I present the D&D cosmology, which quite explicitly has an Astral and Etheric plane, as well as elemental planes, and planes for each of the alignments. Of course, being D&D, these are all "places" material adventurers can go to in order to have adventures (this likely influenced by sci-fi concepts of "alternate dimensions"), but enough of how they're described (such as having a "silver cord" connecting your material body to your astral body) are clearly mined from occult sources, that it seems unlikely to me the influence was any more removed than second-hand.

(Not as directly relevant to the question, but just for completeness: D&D's weird, but rich and engaging, cosmology is further filled out by a lot of default Christian assumptions, like devils, demons, and angels, the Chaos/Order dichotomy made vivid by Poul Anderson and Michael Moorcock, and the Renaissance/Platonic conception of the heavens as nested crystal spheres. All of this got texture and life from smashing in every fantasy and sci-fi concept Gygax, Arneson, and other early contributors could find room for, and subjecting all of it to the necessity of fitting into the rules system, where books like Deities and Demigods gave various beings from both historical myth and more recent pulps, like Thor and Cthulhu, hit points, levels, spells, and all the rest.)

If anyone else knows more for certain, I'd be interested to hear it.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Zodiacal timing
replies.content:
I try to avoid all such debates, because -- appropriately enough for astrology! -- they go round in circles, occasionally doing a retrograde motion, but never actually getting anywhere.

I use tropical astrology for all purposes and I find that it works. Of course your mileage may vary!

replies.title: Re: Zodiacal timing
replies.content:
In my experience, the sign system of vedic astrology is wrong because it actually follows the movement of the constellations due to the precession, which moves the signs a little every year, whereas tropical uses the seasons for the zodiac signs, and thus they're consistent every year.

My theory so far is that astrology is a symbolic simulation model from the perspective of the perceiver, and the symbols need to remain consistent for it to be accurate for prediction. Since the constellations are actually an abstraction, unlike the planets or seasons, their movement introduces error into the predictive model.

So for me, my vedic chart is definitely wrong, whereas the tropical is much more accurate, and the predictions vedic astrologers have given me have been woefully wrong too, whereas tropical predicitons via say transits for example have been much more accurate for me.

comments.title: Interpreting a symbol
comments.content:
Hello dear JMG, and thank you for your good work.

(1) I was wondering if you would have anything interesting to say about the central symbol of the famous Neverending Story book.

It is initially presented as a gold necklace with two serpents, one dark and one light, biting each other's tails, in the shape of an oval. It is called Auryn.

The bearer of this jewel can have any wish granted in the realm of fantasy, and the purpose of this path of (make-believe) wishes is to find one's True Will in the real world. The fantasy creatures will be guided and protected by it, but apparently have no free will of their own.

Near the end of the book, the main character goes inside the Auryn, which is now a large golden cave/hall/dome, where two giant serpents (one white, one black) bite each other's tails, encircling the waters of life.

I understand that the Ouroboros, a single serpent biting its own tail, represents the Astral light. What would change if there are two, one black and one white? What is the connection between this and the two pillars, Joachim and Boaz? Or is it all just random gibberish?

(2) in a related line of thought, what would happen if someone actually decided to follow some random gibberish as a spiritual path? Would it be possible for someone with some innate talent to turn just about anything into a useful path?

Thanks again for your time!

replies.title: Re: Interpreting a symbol
replies.content:
1) The double ouroboros wasn't an invention of Ende. Here's a version from the Aurea Catean Homeri, an important alchemical text:

It's found in alchemical texts back to Hellenistic Greek times, that I know of. It's a symbol of the reconciliation of dualities, closely related to the yin-yang symbol.

2) Half of the pop mysticism of the Sixties can be described that way. It didn't lead anyplace useful.

replies.title: Re: Interpreting a symbol
replies.content: If I may add, it seems from reading The Neverending Story (the book, not the film), that Michael Ende must have known something about occult philosophy and practice.

replies.title: Re: Interpreting a symbol
replies.content:
According to this site - https://michaelende.de/en/author/biography/michael-ende-and-mystical-thinking-0 - he was very interested in Antroposophy, and a few related schools of thinking. I also remember that he was very fond of Japan and its culture - he may have been a Zen practitioner (it's been some years since I read about it, and I can't find the website anymore). His second wife was Japanese.

His views on our culture and economy would've fit very well here: https://userpage.fu-berlin.de/~roehrigw/mittelstaedt/japan-money/Ende_kinen-en.html

replies.title: Re: Interpreting a symbol
replies.content:
I've discussed the Neverending Story on here before, which is something that really resonated with me. As I've said, the movie was always a favorite, and I more recently delved into the book -- again, the english translation by Manheim is decent, albeit flawed, the original German is best if you're capable. The earlier discussion I had on here converged on German Romanticism as a strong influence on the work.

As for the double ouroboros, or AURYN, and its particular relevance to Neverending Story: the central thesis of the work is the relationship between fantasy and reality, and how they interact with each other for better or worse.

One of my gripes with Manheim, despite his overall competence, is that he sometimes glossed over or removed phrases/lines that he seemed to believe were unimportant, yet were highly significant in how they related to the thesis. In particular, the scene between Atreyu and the Childlike Empress originally had more lines elaborating on this. In the virtuous cycle, humans from the real world visit Fantasia and gain valuable insights there, while their creativity & originality contribute to the world of fantasy. Conversely, in the vicious cycle, human apathy/cynicism/plagiarism/etc cause the Nothing, which turns the creatures of Fantasia into lies & delusions, which in turn bring mental blindness to humans instead of insight. Thus, as it says, reality and fantasy can both harm and heal each other.

This ties back into AURYN/double ouroboros, as the two snakes represent reality and fantasy, entwined and feeding off each other, for good or ill.

replies.title: Re: Interpreting a symbol
replies.content:
PS- one of the better reviews I've found online, just thought I'd drop a link:

https://www.stjohnkarp.net/the-neverending-story/

I don't agree on all points, although he has the right idea in broad strokes. In particular, I regard the film as a masterpiece of adaptation, despite the protests of the author, who I believe was wrong about his own work -- just because you wrote an amazing book, doesn't mean you know how to adapt it to film! I could go on at length about the needs of different mediums, and why all the changes and decisions were well-done. But I'll keep it short and say that the author's main complaints are nullified by the film only covering the first half, while the morals he emphasized were centered on the second half.
Really, the two halves feel like two different books, although they're thematically linked and reflect each other. The first half lends itself to action-adventure, which the movie got right, but the second half is reflective and spiritual, more like a Ghibli film. If only there were a proper sequel to speak of, then maybe those complaints would've been resolved -- instead, we got blatant cash-ins worse than the Matrix sequels! But, you can't have it all...

replies.content: Gods, yes. If any of my books get turned into movies I should probably avoid having anything to do with the process, as I don't think like a screenwriter; I probably won't watch the movie that results, for that matter. It's a completely different world.

comments.title: HGA
comments.content:
Good afternoon, Mr. JMG,

To you, what is the Holy Guardian Angel (HGA) as referenced in the Abramelin?

replies.title: Re: HGA
replies.content:
There's a lot of confusion about that. Each of us has an Individuality or higher self, which is the aspect of ourselves that survives the death of the body and passes from life to life; under most circumstances, it is more or less asleep and dreaming during our periods of waking consciousness, but it slowly wakes -- or, more exactly, we slowly wake, and come to know ourselves as our higher selves and not the personalities that temporarily surround them. That's one goal of occult practice.

Each of us also has a spiritual being that is not ourselves -- call it a guardian angel if you favor the Abrahamic terminology, call it the guardian genius if you prefer the Roman Pagan term -- which embodies the will of the Divine toward us. Because we have free will, it can advise us but it cannot make us listen, so most of us blunder along through life ignoring the quiet voice of intuitive guidance that would steer us clear of many disasters and lead us into more constructive paths. That's another goal of occult practice.

Some people use the jargon of the Abramelin working to refer to one of these, some to the other. I don't use the Abramelin jargon at all -- I talk instead of the Individuality and the guardian genius -- so you're free to use "Holy Guardian Angel" for either of these if you like. My point, though, is that both exist and occult training will guide you to work with one or the other, and ultimately with both.

replies.title: Re: HGA
replies.content:
I have a question about this. Where does the higher self reside, ie what plane or body? Our regular incarnations lose the physical, etheric, and astral body on death and a new one is made as we recinarnate, correct?

I ask because some of stuff I've seen in the i ching (such as on this really good resource here: https://jamesdekorne.com/GBCh/GBCh.htm ) describe the higher self / individuality being outside of space time.

Is that correct? Cos that would mean it occupies something even higher than a mental body?



Here's an example from that link:
"Oracle consultation is predicated on the hypothesis that the Self exists outside of the restrictions of space-time. It observes the unfolding life situation from the center, or nucleus of the psyche, and can thus see probabilities that do not yet exist in the physical dimension. In other words, the Self is able to see which choices will result in which consequences. The ego, locked in space-time, only perceives an extremely limited set of probabilities, and has no conception of the long-range consequences of what may appear to be a very insignificant choice. This disparity can obviously cause great stresses in the conduct of the Work because here is where the goals of ego and Self become confused, and it is precisely within this conflict of interests that the often irrational nature of the Self manifests itself. William Butler Yeats was for many years in communication with guiding entities who often exhibited these traits—if we substitute his word “Spirit” with Jung's concept of the “Self,” we receive a frighteningly accurate description of the phenomenon:

The Spirit ... may know the most violent love and hatred possible, for it can see the remote consequences of the most trivial acts of the living, provided those consequences are part of its future life.

As long as the ego makes its choices on the basis of restricted vision and autonomous desires, one's life is largely determined by unconscious fate. When the ego gives up its freedom of choice to the direction of the Self, one begins to attain one's full potential as a human being because it is now following the Self’s intent. However, it is the ego's unique responsibility to maintain some degree of equilibrium between the conflicting demands emerging from the psyche. If we accept Jung's equation of the Self with the god image, then this sometimes demands that the ego have the courage to, in effect, rebel against “God”—without, however, repudiating “His” ultimate purposes or severing contact with the Work. This is the essence of gnostic existentialism and precisely what it's like to be psychologically bashed between the hammer of Heaven and the anvil of Earth."

replies.title: Re: HGA
replies.content:
Okay, let's start with the basics. We have, in incarnation, three and a half bodies: a material body of flesh and blood, an etheric body of life force, an astral body of astral light, and a mental sheath -- it's not yet a mental body, but it will become one eventually. All these are containers for the individuality, so the individuality is above those planes.

The individuality as it exists in us is little more than a seed, formed of the substance of the divine, causal, and spiritual planes. When we began our journey of spiritual evolution, countless ages ago, it was a seed formed of the substance of all seven planes, but so far we've been able to unfold three and a half layers of that seed. When we finish our process of spiritual evolution, countless ages from now, we will have awakened all seven layers, but we will only have three bodies -- mental, spiritual, and causal -- surrounding the essence of the self, which is a spark of the divine plane. We will be able to take on temporary bodies on the astral, etheric, and material planes if we need them.

(In case you were wondering -- we can have three bodies at a time because of our place among the seven great swarms of souls to emanate from the Solar Logos. The first swarm, now the Lords of Flame, have no bodies at all; the second, the Lords of Form, have one body, on the causal plane; the third, the Lords of Mind, have two, on the causal and spiritual planes. The seventh swarm alone, which will begin its pilgrimage through the planes almost unimaginably far in the future, will have six bodies around the divine spark.)

So, yes, the individuality is currently experiencing space and time but its essence comes from beyond space and time, in the divine plane. Your individuality or higher self, however, currently inhabits your three and a half bodies right here in the world of manifestation. It's not something other than you; you are it and it is you -- you just haven't awakened to your true, timeless and spaceless nature yet.

He is somewhat open to the possibility, but he doesn't remember anything. I am not sure what I can do to avoid these negative outcomes; I have not, unfortunately, figured out a way to discuss this with him because part of his pattern is to keep secrets and completely refuse to share.

If you have any suggestions I would appreciate it, I can provide more detail is necessary.

replies.title: Re: Question related to past life
replies.content:
It quite often happens that when a life ends in failure, the soul is reborn into a similar situation -- and often with the same souls that were involved in that failure -- to repeat the experience and get it right this time. If you're in that situation, one way or another, you need to break the pattern.

You can't make your partner break it -- you have neither the power nor the right to make choices for any other soul -- and if he doesn't want to talk about it, you may have no way of knowing whether he's going to break it or not. If you remember what you did and didn't do in that previous life, you know that those choices didn't work. You need to do something different. If you don't, the events will repeat themselves -- over and over again, if it comes to that, in life after life, until the necessary lesson is learned.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If they're smart, they let it happen naturally, when they're ready for it. Otherwise they may find themselves loaded down with hideous or terrifying memories that they have no way to deal with. Past lives aren't necessarily fun! For example, I remember very vividly what it's like to have my head slammed through the windshield in a head-on car crash; I also remember what it feels like to be burned at the stake. (No, it wasn't for witchcraft.) Let it happen, don't make it happen; you'll be glad you waited.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In my case, it was just bits of stray memory. When the first set of memories came through I was working as a nursing assistant at a Seattle nursing home, and one of my jobs was to take a patient out onto the patio when things were quiet and let him smoke one pipe's worth of tobacco. I was helping him, and all of a sudden I got the intense tactile and taste memory of what it's like to smoke a pipe -- something I've never done in this life. It was really vivid, and it stayed in my mind, like a memory. I kept poking at it, trying to remember more while keeping my mind clear of chatter, and very slowly got a scene in which, as an old man, I was sitting in a parlor high up above street level in an urban setting, watching the rain fall, and smoking a pipe. Over time, more bits of memory from that life came through, and memories from other lives followed.

One tip, by the way: most of the people I know who remember anything of their previous lives have done a lot of discursive meditation. It may not work for you, but I'm pretty sure that's what got me to the point of being ready to remember.

I've discussed this before, and I'll reiterate. My past life memories surfaced naturally, in fact I didn't realize what they were at first. They'll come to you when you're ready, in the meantime, spiritual practice helps. Certainly, I've gained greater access to my recollections since delving into the occult, although I got glimpses even before that. My Mom told me that when I was very young, I said things consistent with having drowned on the Titanic; of course being a devout Catholic, my Mom played it off as something I saw on tv (even though I didn't watch much tv as a kid, and especially not in early childhood). The experience of getting into water must've triggered the memory in my young brain, I guess. More recently, the memory surfaced -- electric lights on in the ship, and the feeling of drowning in the dark Atlantic, sucking cold water into my lungs as my consciousness faded out... yeah, that was rough, I'd recommend not drowning if you can help it.

Try not to get too jelly over this, because as you see, it's not all fun & games. As I said, it's whenever you're ready for it. I can tell you that they feel like memories in this life, except with a sense of distance somehow, like there's a pane of glass in-between (hard to describe); there's also a sense of recognition and familiarity, even if it's way outside your experience. In my case, they contain knowledge I didn't have, which I then corroborated, and the details always line up.

comments.title: Questions about the Ego

Last week, you said that the Ego was one's self-perception. What then, is your definition of Ego-Death, and how would one go about achieving it?
-Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Questions about the Ego
replies.content:
I'd define it as a dumb idea.

There's this persistent notion in our world that you have to find the Bad Thing and amputate it, and then you'll be Good. You've got some teachings that consider sex to be the Bad Thing, and some that consider suffering to be the Bad Thing, and some that consider private property to be the Bad Thing, and some that consider ego to be the Bad Thing. The funny thing is, no matter how hard you try to get rid of the Bad Thing, it never goes away. Some of the most egomaniacal people I've ever met insisted that they'd been through ego death, and of course that made them better than everyone else. The No-Ego Ego Trip is quite something to watch!

Wholeness is a saner approach. Instead of trying to get rid of your ego, deflate it and reshape it so it matches who you actually are. Instead of identifying something as the Bad Thing, bring your whole self into balance and harmony.

comments.title: Two Tarot Questions
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG! I hope you are well. Same to the commentariat, too, of course.

Apologies for the long late post, my work schedule is, well, at a bar.

A few months ago, my first long-term, serious girlfriend broke up with me. Naturally, this has been very difficult to deal with, and it has been made worse by an incident wherein she acted in such a way that caused me to believe (not unreasonably, without going into any details,) that we may be getting back together. In any case, I've been doing a lot of thinking, and I recently supplemented it with some tarot divination. I did two readings, and I was hoping to get some guidance on how accurate they are.

I used a three card spread (past/present/future) for these questions. I asked them a few days apart.

1. The first question was as to whether I should simply avoid all romantic relationships in the future. (I'm aware that this may seem like the overreaction of a fairly young man, but this has truly been awful to deal with, and has really derailed parts of my life.)

I got: Past: Page of Wands
Present: The High Preistess
Future: Knight of Pentacles

I interpreted this to mean something like: In the past, I have been very innocent about my approach to relationships, and thus naïve. Now, I must focus on myself, my inner world, and finding what works. In the future, I should use these understandings I've gained in order to steadfastly apply what works to my situation.

2. The second question was whether I should see my ex again to say goodbye (I am moving to another city soon, mostly to get away from places that remind me of our time together.) I do still feel bad about just leaving, though. We haven't talked for a couple of months.

I got: Past: Nine of Wands
Present: Three of Pentacles
Future: Ace of Pentacles

I took this reading to indicate that I have been through many challenges, but that I am still standing. I have also learned things about myself through this. (Sidenote: A little over two years ago I got the Eight of Wands for the future when I asked if I would find love soon, just before I met my ex.) Although I am unsure, my efforts are vital to ensure that things work out, and I need to come together with others (her? Friends?) to achieve my goals. In the future, I have the opportunity to attain something if I work to build on the opportunities which are given to me, and don't remain stuck in my old routines, and ways so rigidly.

Am I on the right track? I do intend to meditate on these further as well.

Thank you so much for all you do.

Duncan

replies.title: Re: Two Tarot Questions
replies.content: Those both seem clear to me; I'd read them more or less the way you have.

replies.title: Re: Two Tarot Questions
replies.content:
Thank you, I'll meditate on how these interpretations relate to my system further.

Duncan

comments.title: Basic initiate questions
comments.content:
Hi there! So I've been interested in working with the subtle energies of reality for years, fell in with the neopagan crowd for a bit, and I have to say that discovering your ecosophia blog has finally helped grasp the outlines a sensible, comprehnsive view of the nonohysical dimensions of reality. I thank you profoundly for it.

So, after buying your translation of Eliphas Levi's book to understand all your blog posts about it, I decided to try and follow along with the discursive meditative system you were laying out. This in turn necessitated pulling out my old tarot deck and looking at it in new ways. Which in turn got me engaging in some divination again. This actally helped settle some very serious anxiety I had about some matters in my life.

This has led me to realize that my spiritual energy has build up a whole lot of crud over the years, and funny enough it wasnot long after that I began exploring this blog and checked out the magic monday faqs, which led me to the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose. And so I've started doing the sphere of protection - and let me tell you, it's been an experience finally clearing out years of azothic trash! That journey alone might take years...

And yet, I feel like I should be doing more. I've created a sacred space, in a flat moss clearing, with crystals pointing towards the 4 cardinal directions, where I go to do the sop ritual and meditate. I am working on the discoursive meditations from your ceremonial magic blog posts, I'm doing astrology and tarot divinations. So what's next? What should my mext steps be along the path that abides?

I dont manage to do any of thwse things more often than weekly. Should I try to make something other than basic meditation a daily practice? I'm insanely busy so time is the shortest of all my comodities. Still, whatever advice or guidance you can give will be welcomed and respected.

Thanks for all your hard work, you truly are a guiding light of clarity in this age of confusion.

replies.title: Re: Basic initiate questions
replies.content: I'm glad that you've found some useful things here. If you want ot add something to your practices, the Sphere of Protection is the next step -- a daily protective ritual of some sort is a very good thing to do, given just how spiritually grubby things are these days. Keep it at that level for at least a year -- a daily meditation, a daily SoP, and divination as often as it works for you -- and you'll have a sense of how it all fits in your schedule and whether it's time to add more work.

comments.title: Interesting book
comments.content:
For anyone interested in the interaction of the occult with the world of literary culture, Susan Johnston Graf's Talking to the Gods: Occultism in the Work of W. B. Yeats, Arthur Machen, Algernon Blackwood, and Dion Fortune is well worth a look.

For the purposes of this book, "the occult" means basically the Golden Dawn tradition, in which all of the listed authors were involved. (The author provides specifics of membership -- dates, places, and so on.) The sequence is quite consciously chosen: Years provides the paradigm case, as it were, against which the others are discussed. In a certain sense, the book builds toward the final chapter, dealing with Dion Fortune, for whose inclusion in a sense the preceding chapters serve as a justification.

This last chapter bears the signs of some difficulty, in that there are a few more editorial problems (typos, etc) and some stray inaccuracies, as though the author were feeling the stress of including a relative literary nonentity in when treating of Yeats, Machen and Blackwood. Still, she provides an interesting reading of Fortune's fiction, and raises some useful points.

She mentions, but does not include, Crowley, who was at least for a while a literary presence. She could also have included some other literary figures, for example Charles Williams, who was closely tied to AE Waite (and thus at just one remove from Machen).

The most interesting chapter is the one on Machen, whose work and life she considers quite carefully. She points out that his famous occult and horror fictions date to a particular period in his life, when he was immersed in a bohemian life with his first wife, whose death from cancer brought this phase of his life to a close (and was associated with his initiation into the GD, which Graf takes as having been prompted by AE Waite's concern for his reaction to his wife's death, and some experiences that came from the "process" Machen implemented to deal with it).

LeGrand
